title: Research open-source and self-hosted architecture options

summary.bottom_line: A shared backend is practical, but the editor UX must be native per editor. VS Code has usable open-source/self-hosted clients now; Obsidian likely needs a custom CodeMirror-based plugin for true inline/tab ghost text.
best_practical_paths: Local-only: Qwen2.5-Coder/StarCoder2/DeepSeek-Coder served by llama.cpp /infill, Ollama, vLLM, or Tabby; VS Code via Continue/Twinny/Tabby; Obsidian via custom plugin.
OpenAI-compatible broker: one /autocomplete service normalizes local and hosted providers, with LiteLLM/OpenRouter/Ollama/vLLM/llama.cpp adapters as needed.
Official API-key path: Mistral Codestral FIM for code autocomplete, or OpenAI/Anthropic/OpenRouter chat APIs as fallback, with separate API billing.
summary.subscription_caveat: ChatGPT and Claude UI subscriptions are not normal API access. Claude Pro/Max can access Claude Code officially, but Claude Code is not a generic low-latency FIM backend for VS Code plus Obsidian.
confidence_notes: High confidence for VS Code/local/API architecture feasibility due to current docs and existing clients.
Medium confidence for Obsidian off-the-shelf availability because this task focused on architecture and implementation path; T030 should verify plugin inventory.
Medium confidence for unofficial proxy assessment because specific proxy repositories were not audited; risk is inferred from official access boundaries.

sources.title: Continue Autocomplete Setup and Configuration Guide
sources.quoted_evidence: Continue documents Ollama local autocomplete setup and says after running qwen2.5-coder and adding it to config, 'you should begin to see completions in VS Code'; it also cautions that thinking models are slower and less suitable for speed-sensitive autocomplete.

sources.title: Twinny README
sources.quoted_evidence: Twinny lists supported providers including 'localhost OpenAI/Ollama Compatible API', OpenAI, Anthropic, and OpenRouter, and lists 'Fill in the Middle Code Completion' with 'AI-powered real-time code suggestions'.

sources.title: Tabby README
sources.quoted_evidence: Tabby describes itself as 'a self-hosted AI coding assistant' and an 'open-source and on-premises alternative to GitHub Copilot', with 'OpenAPI interface' and support for consumer-grade GPUs.

sources.title: Tabby Visual Studio Code Extension Docs
sources.quoted_evidence: The Tabby VS Code page says Tabby is 'open-source' and 'self-hosted' and that 'Tabby suggests multi-line code completions and full functions in real-time as you write code'.

sources.title: Tabby Model Configuration
sources.quoted_evidence: Tabby says its Completion model is for code-completion suggestions and focuses mainly on the Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) prompting style; its Chat model is broadly compatible with OpenAI standards.

sources.title: Ollama OpenAI compatibility
sources.quoted_evidence: Ollama says it provides compatibility with parts of the OpenAI API and documents /v1/chat/completions with supported features including Chat completions and Streaming.

sources.title: Ollama Generate a response API
sources.quoted_evidence: Ollama documents POST /api/generate as generating a response for a provided prompt and shows both default streaming NDJSON behavior and non-streaming with 'stream': false.

sources.title: llama.cpp HTTP Server README
sources.quoted_evidence: The server README lists OpenAI-compatible chat completions, responses, and embeddings, and documents POST /infill 'For code infilling' which 'takes a prefix and a suffix and returns the predicted completion as stream'.

sources.title: vLLM Online Serving
sources.quoted_evidence: vLLM documents an OpenAI-compatible server supporting /v1/completions, /v1/responses, and /v1/chat/completions; for /v1/completions it notes the 'suffix parameter is not supported'.

sources.title: LiteLLM Getting Started
sources.quoted_evidence: LiteLLM describes itself as an open-source library with a unified interface to call 100+ LLMs using the OpenAI format, plus a self-hosted LLM gateway/proxy with virtual keys, cost tracking, and admin UI.

sources.title: OpenRouter Quickstart
sources.quoted_evidence: OpenRouter says it provides a unified API to hundreds of AI models via a single endpoint, documents /api/v1/chat/completions, supports streaming, and can use the OpenAI SDK as a drop-in replacement.

sources.title: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B model card
sources.quoted_evidence: The model card lists license apache-2.0 and says Qwen2.5-Coder can be used for 'fill in the middle tasks'.

sources.title: StarCoder2-7B model card
sources.quoted_evidence: The model card says StarCoder2-7B was trained using the Fill-in-the-Middle objective on 3.5+ trillion tokens and lists license bigcode-openrail-m.

sources.title: DeepSeek Coder 6.7B base model card
sources.quoted_evidence: The model card says the models use a 16K window and an extra fill-in-the-blank task to support project-level code completion and infilling, and includes an FIM-token example.

sources.title: CodeLlama-7B model card
sources.quoted_evidence: The model card lists model capabilities as code completion and infilling, but notes the repository is a non-official mirror and the license is llama2.

sources.title: Mistral coding and FIM documentation
sources.quoted_evidence: Mistral documents Codestral as specifically trained for Code Generation and FIM and documents /v1/fim/completions as a Fill-in-the-middle endpoint for code completion and generation supporting codestral-latest.

sources.title: How can I move my ChatGPT subscription to the API?
sources.quoted_evidence: OpenAI Help says the API service is billed and managed separately to ChatGPT, can be upgraded to pay-as-you-go by adding a payment method in API billing settings, and API usage is charged by tokens used.

sources.title: Paid Claude subscription versus Claude API and Console
sources.quoted_evidence: Claude Help says Claude paid plans and Claude Console are separate products; a paid Claude subscription enhances chat but doesn't include access to the Claude API or Console, and API usage requires separately setting up Console access.

sources.title: Claude API access and billing help
sources.quoted_evidence: Claude Help says the Claude Console is where users create API keys, add users, set up billing, and experiment with Workbench.

sources.title: Use Claude Code with your Pro or Max plan
sources.quoted_evidence: Claude Help says Pro and Max subscriptions can access Claude Code in the terminal with one unified subscription; it also notes an ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable makes Claude Code use API charges instead of subscription usage.

sources.title: VS Code vscode.d.ts InlineCompletionItemProvider
sources.quoted_evidence: VS Code's API declares InlineCompletionItemProvider for inline completions requested implicitly while typing and declares languages.registerInlineCompletionItemProvider to register such providers.

sources.title: Obsidian API TypeScript definitions
sources.quoted_evidence: The Obsidian API exposes registerEditorExtension(extension: Extension) for CodeMirror 6 extensions and defines EditorSuggest<T> extending PopoverSuggest<T> for editor suggestion UIs.

sources.title: CodeMirror Reference Manual - Decoration and WidgetType
sources.quoted_evidence: CodeMirror documents Decoration.widget as creating a widget decoration that displays a DOM element at a position; positive side draws after the cursor at the same position, and inline widget is the default.

claims.text: For low-latency inline autocomplete, FIM-capable base/code models are architecturally preferable to generic chat-only prompting because the editor naturally has both prefix and suffix around the cursor.
claims.notes: The sources directly document FIM model training or FIM endpoints; the preference for editor autocomplete is an inference from cursor prefix/suffix context.

claims.text: VS Code has practical open-source or free clients that already provide inline/live code completion against local or API endpoints, especially Continue, Twinny, and Tabby.
claims.notes: T020 should still compare marketplace quality, but T040 does not need a custom VS Code extension for the common local/API architectures.

claims.text: If existing VS Code clients are insufficient, VS Code exposes a first-class InlineCompletionItemProvider API for ghost-text completions requested while typing.
claims.notes: This supports a custom VS Code adapter to the same shared backend used by Obsidian.

claims.text: A true Obsidian Copilot-like inline/tab autocomplete path is feasible but likely requires a custom plugin: Obsidian allows registering CodeMirror 6 extensions, and CodeMirror widget decorations can draw inline ghost text at the cursor.
claims.notes: This is an implementation feasibility claim, not a claim that an off-the-shelf Obsidian plugin already provides this UX; T030 should verify plugin inventory.

claims.text: Tabby is the most turnkey self-hosted code-completion stack for VS Code because it bundles a server, completion model role, and VS Code extension, but it does not by itself solve Obsidian true inline autocomplete.
claims.notes: A custom Obsidian plugin could still call Tabby or a companion broker if the API is suitable.

claims.text: Ollama is a practical local front door for local models because it exposes a local HTTP API, streaming generation, and partial OpenAI-compatible chat completions.
claims.notes: The cited docs do not document a dedicated FIM endpoint; clients must use chat/generate prompts or model-specific FIM tokens.

claims.text: llama.cpp server is a strong local backend for custom autocomplete brokers because it has both OpenAI-compatible routes and a dedicated streaming /infill endpoint accepting prefix and suffix.
claims.notes: The /infill endpoint is non-OpenAI-compatible, so a broker may be needed for clients that only speak OpenAI chat/completions.

claims.text: vLLM is attractive for GPU throughput and OpenAI-compatible serving, but the documented OpenAI completions route does not support the suffix parameter, so exact OpenAI-style FIM is not first-class through that endpoint.
claims.notes: FIM may still be possible by manually formatting model-specific FIM tokens in the prompt, but that is not the same as a standard suffix field.

claims.text: OpenAI-compatible gateways are useful for sharing one backend between editors, but autocomplete-specific FIM behavior is not standardized across Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM, Mistral FIM, LiteLLM, and OpenRouter.
claims.notes: A small custom broker with a normalized /autocomplete contract is safer than exposing raw provider differences to every editor plugin.

claims.text: LiteLLM can centralize provider routing, keys, budgets, and OpenAI-format calls, but it is not itself an autocomplete model or FIM engine.
claims.notes: It is best used behind a broker or as the broker's provider gateway.

claims.text: OpenRouter is useful as a hosted OpenAI-compatible model gateway with streaming, but it is an API-key architecture rather than a way to spend a ChatGPT or Claude UI subscription.
claims.notes: OpenRouter documents its own unified API; no source found tying it to UI subscriptions.

claims.text: A ChatGPT subscription should not be treated as OpenAI API access for an autocomplete backend; OpenAI says API service is billed and managed separately from ChatGPT.
claims.notes: This does not preclude separate OpenAI API pay-as-you-go keys.

claims.text: A Claude paid plan does not include Claude API/Console access, although Pro/Max can officially access Claude Code as a terminal coding product under the same subscription.
claims.notes: Claude Code is relevant but not a generic low-latency FIM endpoint for VS Code plus Obsidian.

claims.text: Unofficial session/cookie/browser proxies are a poor foundation for a cross-editor autocomplete product because they rely on non-API surfaces while the official vendors distinguish UI subscriptions from API access.
claims.notes: This handoff did not audit specific unofficial proxy repositories; the risk rating is based on supported access boundaries and likely brittleness/account-policy exposure.

claims.text: A shared backend can serve both VS Code and Obsidian if it exposes a normalized autocomplete API, but each editor still needs a native client to collect context, debounce requests, render ghost text, and accept with Tab.
claims.notes: This is the key architecture recommendation for cross-editor support.

options.name: Local-only FIM-capable backend with editor clients

access_model.access_notes: No external account or UI subscription is needed unless optional cloud models are enabled.

strengths: Best privacy posture and no per-token API bill.
Can use small, fast code models such as Qwen2.5-Coder locally.
VS Code can use existing clients; Obsidian can call the same local broker.
limitations: Requires local CPU/GPU capacity; quality and latency depend heavily on hardware and model size.
Obsidian true inline UX requires custom plugin work.
Generic OpenAI-compatible local APIs do not standardize FIM; broker must know model/server-specific FIM format.
required_components: Ollama or llama.cpp or vLLM or Tabby
VS Code client such as Continue/Twinny/Tabby or custom extension

vscode.rationale: Continue, Twinny, and Tabby all provide practical VS Code autocomplete paths against local/self-hosted endpoints.

obsidian.rationale: Feasible via Obsidian CodeMirror extension and widget decorations, but no ready T040-verified packaged true-inline plugin.

options.name: Official API-key backend using provider APIs

access_model.access_notes: Supported and stable, but ChatGPT and Claude UI subscriptions do not cover ordinary API usage; Claude Pro/Max's Claude Code is near-scope but not a generic autocomplete API.

strengths: No local GPU requirement and can use strong hosted models.
Mistral Codestral provides a purpose-built FIM API for code completion.
Provider APIs generally support streaming and production authentication/rate limits.
limitations: Separate API billing and key management are required for OpenAI/Anthropic-style APIs.
OpenAI/Anthropic chat APIs are not inherently FIM endpoints; quality may be worse than code-specific FIM models for code autocomplete.
Remote calls add privacy, latency, quota, and cost concerns for every keystroke-adjacent request.
required_components: autocomplete broker to shape context and cap tokens

vscode.rationale: Existing clients can use provider APIs or OpenAI-compatible gateways; Codestral/FIM-capable endpoints are most appropriate.

obsidian.rationale: Backend is straightforward, but editor integration and UX must be built or found in T030.

options.name: OpenAI-compatible proxy or gateway shared by both editors

access_model.access_notes: Billing depends on upstream: local-only can be no-bill, hosted APIs require separate billing. Proxy compatibility is useful but does not guarantee FIM feature parity.

strengths: Single base URL and auth layer can serve VS Code and Obsidian.
Lets the backend switch among local models, OpenRouter, OpenAI, Anthropic-compatible adapters, or Mistral without changing editor plugins.
Good place to centralize budgets, caching, rate limiting, telemetry policy, and secret storage.
limitations: OpenAI-compatible chat/completions is not a complete autocomplete standard.
Provider-specific FIM endpoints may need custom routes outside /v1/chat/completions.
If every editor talks directly to a generic proxy, autocomplete context shaping and stale-request cancellation tend to be duplicated.

vscode.rationale: Continue/Twinny-style clients already understand many OpenAI-compatible endpoints.

obsidian.rationale: A plugin can call a single proxy/broker, but the inline UI remains custom work.

options.name: Unofficial ChatGPT/Claude subscription or session proxy

access_model.access_notes: Would depend on non-API UI/session surfaces or reverse-engineered adapters. This is distinct from official Claude Code, which is a terminal coding product but not a generic FIM API.

strengths: Potentially aligns with the user's wish to reuse an existing UI subscription, if it works at all.
Could theoretically expose a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint to both editors.
limitations: Unsupported, brittle, likely policy/account-risky, and difficult to verify or maintain.
No evidence of a stable, low-latency FIM surface suitable for keystroke-level autocomplete.
Obsidian still needs custom plugin work; VS Code clients may work only if the proxy perfectly emulates expected endpoints.

vscode.rationale: Technically possible if a proxy emulates OpenAI-compatible completions, but unsupported access and latency make it a poor recommendation.

obsidian.rationale: Adds all VS Code risks plus custom plugin work and weak FIM/streaming guarantees.

options.name: Tabby self-hosted coding assistant

access_model.access_notes: No external account or UI subscription is needed unless optional cloud models are enabled.

strengths: Turnkey self-hosted code assistant
VS Code extension documented
FIM-oriented completion model role
OpenAPI interface for integration
limitations: Primarily code/editor-extension focused; no documented Obsidian true-inline plugin in this handoff
May be overkill if only a simple Obsidian note-completion backend is needed

vscode.rationale: Official VS Code extension and realtime multiline completions are documented.

obsidian.rationale: Potential backend only; custom plugin and API compatibility testing required.

options.name: General local model servers: llama.cpp, Ollama, or vLLM

access_model.access_notes: No external account or UI subscription is needed unless optional cloud models are enabled.

strengths: Flexible model choice
Can run on local hardware
Can be hidden behind a shared broker
llama.cpp gives a dedicated infill API; Ollama is easy to operate; vLLM scales better on GPUs
limitations: Different endpoint semantics
FIM formatting must be model/server-specific
vLLM's OpenAI completions route lacks suffix support

vscode.rationale: Existing VS Code clients can call local endpoints, especially OpenAI-compatible ones.

obsidian.rationale: Good backend for a custom plugin; no off-the-shelf UX guaranteed by this task.

options.name: Mistral Codestral FIM API

access_model.access_notes: Official code-FIM API; pricing/key requirements are separate from ChatGPT/Claude subscriptions and should be verified in synthesis if chosen.

strengths: Purpose-built FIM endpoint
Supports code completion and code generation
Good fit for code autocomplete if remote API is acceptable
limitations: Code-specialized, not necessarily best for prose notes
Remote API cost/privacy/latency
Not an existing Obsidian UX

vscode.rationale: VS Code autocomplete clients or a custom extension can call a broker that maps to /v1/fim/completions.

obsidian.rationale: Usable from a custom plugin/broker, especially for code blocks; prose quality should be tested.

architecture_patterns.name: Shared autocomplete broker behind editor-native clients
architecture_patterns.summary: Recommended cross-editor pattern: both VS Code and Obsidian call one local or hosted /autocomplete service that normalizes editor context into provider-specific FIM/chat requests.
components: VS Code client: Continue/Twinny/Tabby where possible, or custom InlineCompletionItemProvider
Obsidian plugin: CodeMirror extension with ghost-text rendering and Tab accept
Broker API: debounced requests, cancellation tokens/request ids, prefix/suffix extraction, stop tokens, caching, provider routing
Provider adapters: llama.cpp /infill, Ollama generate/chat, vLLM completions/chat, Mistral FIM, LiteLLM/OpenRouter/OpenAI-compatible chat
why_it_fits: One backend can enforce privacy, rate limits, prompt shaping, and model selection for both editors.
Editor-specific code remains small and only handles UX/context capture.
It avoids assuming that OpenAI-compatible chat is enough for all FIM cases.
risks: Broker must handle stale responses and per-editor cursor ranges carefully
More initial custom work than direct VS Code-only setup

architecture_patterns.name: Use ready VS Code client, build only Obsidian plugin
architecture_patterns.summary: Lowest custom-code path for cross-editor local/API autocomplete: configure Continue/Twinny/Tabby in VS Code, then build an Obsidian plugin to call the same model server or broker.
components: Continue or Twinny or Tabby in VS Code
why_it_fits: VS Code side is already solved well enough for most open-source/local architectures
Custom effort focuses on the missing Obsidian true-inline UX
risks: Different clients may shape prompts differently; accepted suggestions can diverge by editor
If using Tabby-specific APIs, Obsidian integration requires API inspection/testing

architecture_patterns.name: Editors call OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly
architecture_patterns.summary: Simple but less robust: configure clients/plugins to call /v1/chat/completions or /v1/completions on Ollama, vLLM, LiteLLM, OpenRouter, or similar.
components: client-side prompt builder in each editor
API key storage in each editor
why_it_fits: Fastest prototype if the chosen client already supports custom base URLs
risks: Duplicates secret handling and prompt logic
FIM suffix handling is not portable
Harder to enforce cross-editor privacy/cost limits

architecture_patterns.name: Subscription/session proxy exposing a fake API
architecture_patterns.summary: Not recommended: use ChatGPT/Claude web session or an app-login wrapper to present a local API to editors.
why_it_fits: Only potentially fits the desire to reuse a UI subscription without API billing
risks: Unsupported access boundary
brittleness
policy/account risk
latency unsuitable for live autocomplete
weak or absent FIM semantics

vscode.fit: Existing clients plus local model servers make this practical today.
vscode.latency: good if small model/hardware is adequate; streaming and short completions help.

obsidian.fit: Backend is practical, but true inline/tab UX requires custom plugin.
obsidian.latency: good if plugin debounces, cancels stale requests, and limits max tokens.

feasibility_ratings.subscription_reuse: not_applicable; does not use GPT/Claude subscriptions.

vscode.fit: Supported by existing VS Code clients and custom extension APIs.
vscode.latency: mixed; hosted model/network speed and rate limits matter.

obsidian.fit: API calls are straightforward, but inline rendering/acceptance is custom.
obsidian.latency: mixed; needs debounce, request cancellation, token caps, and possibly non-streaming first-line responses.

feasibility_ratings.subscription_reuse: poor for ChatGPT/Claude UI plans; API keys and separate billing are the supported route. Claude Pro/Max Claude Code is official but not a generic autocomplete endpoint.

vscode.fit: Many VS Code clients accept custom OpenAI-compatible base URLs.
vscode.latency: good to mixed depending on upstream; gateway adds minimal overhead but upstream may dominate.

obsidian.fit: Good backend abstraction for a plugin; no built-in Obsidian UX solved by proxy alone.
obsidian.latency: good to mixed; plugin should call a normalized autocomplete endpoint rather than raw chat when possible.

feasibility_ratings.subscription_reuse: poor for ChatGPT/Claude UI subscriptions; can route official API keys or local models.

vscode.fit: Only works if a fragile proxy emulates the expected API; not recommended.
vscode.latency: poor to unknown; session/web surfaces are not designed for keystroke-level autocomplete.

obsidian.fit: Same risks plus custom plugin effort.

feasibility_ratings.subscription_reuse: theoretically yes for UI subscriptions, but unsupported. Official Claude Code with Pro/Max is near-scope but not a stable generic FIM backend.

fim_capable_models.access: open weights; Qwen2.5-Coder-7B model card lists Apache-2.0
fim_capable_models.fim_evidence: Model card says it can be used for fill in the middle tasks; Continue recommends qwen2.5-coder:1.5b for local autocomplete with Ollama.
fim_capable_models.autocomplete_fit: high for local code autocomplete; 1.5B/7B are realistic latency candidates.

fim_capable_models.access: open weights under BigCode OpenRAIL-M terms
fim_capable_models.fim_evidence: StarCoder2-7B model card says it was trained using the Fill-in-the-Middle objective.
fim_capable_models.autocomplete_fit: high for code-oriented local autocomplete, subject to license suitability and hardware.

fim_capable_models.access: open weights under DeepSeek license; check commercial terms before productizing
fim_capable_models.fim_evidence: Model card says models include a fill-in-the-blank task for project-level code completion and infilling and shows FIM tokens.
fim_capable_models.autocomplete_fit: high for code if license and serving stack are acceptable.

fim_capable_models.fim_evidence: Model card lists code completion and infilling capabilities.
fim_capable_models.autocomplete_fit: medium; older but still serviceable, license and quality should be weighed against Qwen/StarCoder/DeepSeek alternatives.

fim_capable_models.access: official Mistral API; local/open-weight licensing not assessed in this task
fim_capable_models.fim_evidence: Mistral docs say Codestral is trained for code generation and FIM and expose /v1/fim/completions.
fim_capable_models.autocomplete_fit: high for API-key code autocomplete; remote latency/cost/privacy apply.

serving_stacks.role: easy local model runner and OpenAI-compatible chat/generate endpoint
serving_stacks.fim_support: indirect; use model-specific FIM prompt/tokens or client prompt shaping, because cited Ollama docs cover generate/chat streaming but not a dedicated FIM endpoint
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: supports streaming in generate and OpenAI-compatible chat; good for small local models
serving_stacks.best_fit: single-user local autocomplete prototype and Continue/Twinny custom-base-url workflows
risks: FIM semantics not standardized
model templates may not preserve suffix exactly

serving_stacks.role: lightweight local CPU/GPU server for quantized models
serving_stacks.fim_support: direct /infill endpoint with prefix/suffix and streaming; also OpenAI-compatible routes for generic clients
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: good for quantized small/medium models; /infill stream is a strong fit for editor autocomplete
serving_stacks.best_fit: custom shared broker that calls /infill for FIM models
risks: /infill is not OpenAI-compatible, so generic clients need an adapter
model-specific FIM token order may matter

serving_stacks.fim_support: partial/indirect; OpenAI /v1/completions suffix parameter is documented as unsupported, so broker must use model-specific FIM prompt formatting or another route
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: excellent for batched GPU serving; overkill for one small local CPU-only setup
serving_stacks.best_fit: team/shared GPU backend or multiple editors/users
risks: suffix gap for FIM
requires stronger hardware/ops than Ollama or llama.cpp

serving_stacks.role: turnkey self-hosted coding assistant with server and editor extensions
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: designed for real-time VS Code completions according to docs; local hardware still matters
serving_stacks.best_fit: VS Code self-hosted Copilot replacement; possible backend component for a custom Obsidian plugin after API testing
risks: primarily code-focused
Obsidian integration not built-in

serving_stacks.role: self-hosted API gateway/proxy with OpenAI-format normalization, virtual keys, spend tracking, and routing
serving_stacks.fim_support: not a model/FIM endpoint itself; use upstream FIM where available or custom routes
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: gateway overhead should be small; upstream dominates
serving_stacks.best_fit: centralize keys, quotas, logging policy, and provider routing behind a broker
risks: least-common-denominator chat API can hide FIM-specific provider features

serving_stacks.fim_support: model-dependent and not guaranteed by the quickstart; best treated as chat/completions gateway unless a selected model documents FIM
serving_stacks.streaming_or_latency: supports streaming; latency/cost vary by routed provider/model
serving_stacks.best_fit: API-key experimentation across many hosted models without self-hosting
risks: not tied to GPT/Claude UI subscriptions
model capability and retention/privacy policies vary

shared_backend_assessment.assessment: The same model server or broker can serve both VS Code and Obsidian, but the editor-side UX cannot be shared wholesale. VS Code can rely on Continue/Twinny/Tabby or a custom InlineCompletionItemProvider; Obsidian needs a CodeMirror-based plugin for true inline/tab suggestions unless T030 finds an existing plugin with that exact UX.

recommended_backend_contract.notes: Keep the editor plugin provider-agnostic; all FIM token formatting and provider quirks belong in the broker.

recommended_provider_order_for_prototype: Local llama.cpp /infill with Qwen2.5-Coder or StarCoder2 for deterministic FIM testing
Ollama qwen2.5-coder via Continue/Twinny for quick VS Code proof of life
Mistral Codestral /v1/fim/completions for hosted code-FIM comparison
LiteLLM/OpenRouter only after deciding API-key billing is acceptable
shared_backend_assessment.why_not_raw_chat_only: Raw chat/completions can work for prose and simple continuations, but code autocomplete benefits from suffix-aware FIM and strict latency/token caps; chat-only prompts are a fallback, not the best default.

obsidian_custom_plugin_path.objective: Implement true inline ghost-text autocomplete in Obsidian against the same backend used by VS Code.

minimum_viable_design.name: Plugin shell and settings
minimum_viable_design.details: Create an Obsidian TypeScript plugin with settings for backend URL, optional API key or local-only mode, enabled file types, max tokens, debounce delay, and privacy exclusions. Keep remote provider keys in a local broker when possible rather than in the vault plugin.

minimum_viable_design.name: Register CodeMirror extension
minimum_viable_design.details: Use Plugin.registerEditorExtension with a CodeMirror 6 ViewPlugin/update listener and state field to track cursor, current suggestion, loading state, and request id.

minimum_viable_design.name: Context extraction
minimum_viable_design.details: On idle typing with an empty selection, collect prefix and suffix around the cursor under a token/character budget; include language hints for fenced code blocks, note path/title, and optional nearby headings. Avoid whole-vault context in the first version.

minimum_viable_design.name: Debounced cancellable request
minimum_viable_design.details: After roughly 150-350 ms of inactivity, POST to /autocomplete with prefix, suffix, editor='obsidian', file metadata, and max_tokens around 32-128. Use AbortController/request ids so stale responses never render.

minimum_viable_design.name: Backend normalization
minimum_viable_design.details: Broker maps the request to llama.cpp /infill, Mistral /v1/fim/completions, Ollama generate/chat, OpenAI-compatible chat, or another adapter. Use temperature 0 or low temperature and stop tokens such as blank-line/heading boundaries where appropriate.

minimum_viable_design.name: Ghost text rendering
minimum_viable_design.details: Render only the leading useful span as muted inline text with CodeMirror Decoration.widget at the cursor. Prefer single-line display first; allow multiline only after UX testing. Clear on document change, cursor movement, or Escape.

minimum_viable_design.name: Accept/reject keymap
minimum_viable_design.details: Install a keymap where Tab accepts the current suggestion by dispatching an insertion transaction at the recorded range; Esc clears; optionally Ctrl/Alt+Bracket cycles suggestions. Do not override Tab when no suggestion is visible or when Obsidian lists/indentation should win.

minimum_viable_design.name: Fallback popup mode
minimum_viable_design.details: If ghost text proves fragile, use EditorSuggest as a completion-popup fallback; this is easier but less Copilot-like than inline ghost text.

minimum_viable_design.name: Safety and observability
minimum_viable_design.details: Add visible local/remote indicator, per-vault enablement, excluded folders, request logging disabled by default, and a panic toggle. Track latency and acceptance rate locally for model tuning.

backend_request_contract.notes: Keep the editor plugin provider-agnostic; all FIM token formatting and provider quirks belong in the broker.

risks.risk: Autocomplete is keystroke-adjacent; models that feel fine in chat can feel unusable inline.
risks.mitigation: Use small FIM models, low temperature, max_tokens 32-128, debounce/cancel, cache prompt prefixes, and render only after cursor remains stable.

risks.risk: OpenAI-compatible endpoints do not standardize FIM; vLLM documents no suffix support for /v1/completions while Mistral and llama.cpp use distinct FIM endpoints.
risks.mitigation: Hide provider quirks behind a broker and maintain per-provider adapters/tests.

risks.risk: Users may conflate ChatGPT/Claude UI subscriptions with API access; official docs say these are separate for normal API usage.
risks.mitigation: State clearly that official autocomplete backends require local models or separate API billing, except near-scope Claude Code subscription workflows that are not generic autocomplete APIs.

risks.risk: Obsidian editing semantics differ from code editors: Markdown lists, tables, backlinks, commands, and IME input can conflict with Tab acceptance.
risks.mitigation: Make Tab override conditional, provide Escape, disable in excluded contexts, and offer popup fallback.

risks.risk: Remote API/autocomplete can leak note/code context repeatedly as the user types.
risks.mitigation: Default to local models when privacy matters; add vault/folder exclusions, visible remote indicator, and minimal context windows.

risks.risk: Open-weight models are not uniformly OSI open-source or commercially permissive.
risks.mitigation: Prefer Apache-2.0 Qwen2.5-Coder for permissive local experiments; verify StarCoder2, DeepSeek, CodeLlama, and Codestral terms before redistribution or commercial use.

risks.risk: Unofficial subscription/session proxies may break, violate access expectations, or put accounts at risk.
risks.mitigation: Do not recommend for the final practical path; if mentioned, label as unsupported personal experimentation only.

recommendations_for_synthesis: Treat local-only and OpenAI-compatible-proxy architectures as the best-supported open-source paths; use a shared broker for cross-editor consistency.

For VS Code, prefer existing clients first: Continue/Twinny for flexible endpoints or Tabby for turnkey self-hosting. Only build a custom VS Code extension if these cannot meet UX or backend requirements.

For Obsidian, expect custom plugin work for true inline/tab ghost text unless T030 finds a current off-the-shelf plugin with that exact UX. The plugin should use CodeMirror decorations and a conditional Tab keymap.

Do not present ChatGPT or Claude UI subscriptions as official API access. Mention Claude Pro/Max Claude Code as a relevant official subscription-based coding product, but not as a generic shared autocomplete backend.
If remote API billing is acceptable, Mistral Codestral FIM is more autocomplete-shaped for code than generic chat APIs; for prose notes, compare quality empirically against general chat models.
Avoid final-report recommendations based on unofficial session proxies except as a clearly discouraged experimental path.
